---
title: "An Introduction to `dplyr`"
author: "Andy Grogan-Kaylor"
date: "2026-03-30"
categories: [stats, R]
image: "plyers.png"
---

# Background

`dplyr` is a very powerful R library for managing and processing data.[^dplyr]

[^dplyr]: The origins of the name `dplyr` seem somewhat obscure, but I sometimes think of this package as the *data plyers*.

While `dplyr` is very powerful, learning to use `dplyr` can be very confusing. This blog post aims to present some of the most common `dplyr` functions and commands in the form of a brief cheatsheet.


::: {.cell}

:::




::: {.cell}

:::


# Simulated Data


::: {.cell}

:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

---------------------------
 year   x       y       z  
------ ---- --------- -----
 2019   NA   Group B   90  

 2020   50   Group C   90  

 2020   NA   Group A   100 

 2021   70   Group A   110 

 2021   80   Group B   120 
---------------------------


:::
:::


# Piping

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key Idea
*Pipes* `%>%` connect pieces of a command e.g. *data* to *data wrangling* to a *graph command.*
:::

`dplyr` commands will often look something like the outline below.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mydata %>% 
  data_wrangling %>% 
  more_data_wrangling %>%
  graph_command
```
:::



# Aggregate Data: `group_by()` & `summarise()`

:::{.callout-tip}
## `NA` Values

Notice how when aggregating data, we need to be explicit about whether we are removing `NA` values.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  group_by(year) %>% # group by y
  summarise(mean_x = mean(x, 
                          na.rm = TRUE), # mean of x; removing NA's
            n = n()) # count up
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

-------------------
 year   mean_x   n 
------ -------- ---
 2019     NA     1 

 2020     50     2 

 2021     75     2 
-------------------


:::
:::


# Select A Subset of Variables: `select()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  select(x,y) # select only x and y
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

--------------
 x       y    
---- ---------
 NA   Group B 

 50   Group C 

 NA   Group A 

 70   Group A 

 80   Group B 
--------------


:::
:::


# Filter A Subset of Rows: `filter()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  filter(year > 2020) # filter on year
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

---------------------------
 year   x       y       z  
------ ---- --------- -----
 2021   70   Group A   110 

 2021   80   Group B   120 
---------------------------


:::
:::


# Create New Variables: `mutate()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  mutate(myscale = x + z) # create a new variable e.g. a scale
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

-------------------------------------
 year   x       y       z    myscale 
------ ---- --------- ----- ---------
 2019   NA   Group B   90      NA    

 2020   50   Group C   90      140   

 2020   NA   Group A   100     NA    

 2021   70   Group A   110     180   

 2021   80   Group B   120     200   
-------------------------------------


:::
:::


# Recode Variables: `mutate()`

## Continuous Into Categorical: `mutate()` & `cut()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  mutate(zcategorical = cut(z, # cut at breaks
                            breaks=c(-Inf, 100, Inf), 
               labels = c("low", "high")))
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

------------------------------------------
 year   x       y       z    zcategorical 
------ ---- --------- ----- --------------
 2019   NA   Group B   90        low      

 2020   50   Group C   90        low      

 2020   NA   Group A   100       low      

 2021   70   Group A   110       high     

 2021   80   Group B   120       high     
------------------------------------------


:::
:::


## Categorical Into Categorical: `mutate()` & `recode()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mynewdata <- mydata %>% 
  mutate(yrecoded = dplyr::recode(y, # recode values
                         "Group A" = "Red Group",
                         "Group B" = "Blue Group", 
                         .default = "Other"))
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

----------------------------------------
 year   x       y       z     yrecoded  
------ ---- --------- ----- ------------
 2019   NA   Group B   90    Blue Group 

 2020   50   Group C   90      Other    

 2020   NA   Group A   100   Red Group  

 2021   70   Group A   110   Red Group  

 2021   80   Group B   120   Blue Group 
----------------------------------------


:::
:::


# Rename Variables: `rename()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
newdata <- mydata %>% 
  rename(age = x, # rename
         mental_health = z)
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

--------------------------------------
 year   age      y      mental_health 
------ ----- --------- ---------------
 2019   NA    Group B        90       

 2020   50    Group C        90       

 2020   NA    Group A        100      

 2021   70    Group A        110      

 2021   80    Group B        120      
--------------------------------------


:::
:::


# Drop Missing Values: `filter()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
newdata <- mydata %>% 
  filter(!is.na(x)) # filter by x is not missing
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

---------------------------
 year   x       y       z  
------ ---- --------- -----
 2020   50   Group C   90  

 2021   70   Group A   110 

 2021   80   Group B   120 
---------------------------


:::
:::


# Random Sample:  `sample_frac()`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
newdata <- mydata %>% 
  sample_frac(.5) # fraction of data to sample
```
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

---------------------------
 year   x       y       z  
------ ---- --------- -----
 2021   70   Group A   110 

 2019   NA   Group B   90  
---------------------------


:::
:::


# Connecting To Other Packages Like `ggplot`

Notice how, in the code below, I never actually create the new data set `mynewdata`.  I simply pipe `mydata` into a `dplyr` command, and pipe the result directly to `ggplot2`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)

mydata %>% # my data
  mutate(myscale = x + z) %>% # dplyr command to make new variable 
  filter(y != "Group C") %>% # filter on values of y
  ggplot(aes(x = year, # the rest is ggplot
             fill = y)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() + # viridis colors
  theme_minimal() + # minimal theme
  labs(title = "Group by Year") + # labels
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10, # tweak theme
                                   angle = 90))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::
